This
letter
is
sent
to

Contained in a letter dated April 30,

Acceptance corporation, which is wholly owned oy General

southern Californla Gas company {Socal G357 has filed a
request for a rate increase of soon to send million
dollars.
Based on a staff proposal,
the cane decided to
determine the rate design or structure issues prior to
ruling on the rate increase itselfr
an Administrative Law
Judge has conducted hearings on the rate design issues and
has prepared a draft decision for cpuc consideration.
commissioner Grew is the "assigned" commissioner for this

responsible for coordinating commission consideration or
the matter.
she will play a major role in shaping the
final decision.
During the hearings, two major proposals for socal
Gas's rate design were presented.
One proposal came from
SoCal Gas
itself,
and the other came
from CPLVC
staff.
During the hearings, several persons made comments on the
proposals.
one or the participants was a representative
from General Motors.
when Commissioner Grew became
aware
of General Motors'
participation, she requested from you, and you in turn
requested from us, advice as to whether she had a conflict
of interest under the Political Reform Act.
We had several
phone discussions regarding this matter during which you
provided me with the following additional infarmatiunr
socal Gas serves approximately 4 million customers in

industrial customers.
The current
rate
structure
for

Gas sets up three tiers for residential rates based upon
the number of
therms per month
used.
Commercial and
industrial customers pay a set amount per therm used which
is equivalent to the second tier residential rate.
Customers are also divided on the basis or peak daily

5N1 customers are those

c

who use
100 million cubic
feet

or
less

over luu mcr. 2/
These classifications are used to
determine priority of usage when shortages occur.
GNl
customers have the highest priority, and so forth.
The staff proposal for socal Gas Co,'s rate design
would
set up two
tiers
for commercial and
industrial
customers who are nonresidential Gui or GNZ users based

therms used per month would be 5.05 lower
than
the
rate
for
all
therms used over
300
therms.
The
rate for the therms used over 300 would he the same as the
tier
2 residential rate.
In
addition
to all residential users.
GNl
includes
179,055 commercial users and 29,391 industrial users.
6N2
has 270 commercial users, and as; industrial users.
a
large number of Gul customers use under 300 therms per
month. All cm customers use over 300 therms per month, and
the majority use over 2,500 therms per month.
Commercial
and
industrial customers used
a total of
6,4A4,000,000 therms in 1930 which is approximately one of
the
total
number of
therms of
10,779,000,000.
However:
these customers account for approximately 542 or the
utility-s revenue,i/ and they comprise approximately 5.7i
of all customers,
General Motors is a GM customer which uses over 2,500
therms per month at its racilities located in Southern
California Within SoCal 635‘:
service area.
ANALYSIS
Government Code Section anon provides:
i/
GN2 customers also cannot use an alternate fuel
to gas.
There are other customers
(GN3VGNS‘;
who can
use
alternate
fuels.
1/
Annual sales in 1980 to commercial and industrial
Customers
totalled $3r327IOOU.DDU;
total
revenues were
55,2lZVOUU,OUUi
1980
is
the most recent year
for
which
figures are availaole.


Steve weissman
May 13, 1982
Page 4
No public official at any level of state or local
government shall make. participate in making or in any
way attempt to use his official position to influence a
governmental decision in which he knows or has reason
to know he has a financial interest.
*
An official has a financial interest in a governmental
decision when it is reasonably foreseeable that there will
be a material financial effect on an investment or interest
in real property of the official, or a source of income to
the official, which is distinguishable from the effect on
the public generally.
section 37103.
Since commissioner
Grew has an investment of more than $1,000 in General
Motors, she may not participate in any decision which could
foreseeahly have a material financial effect on General
Motors.
since it is reasonably foreseeable that the proposed
changes to the utility's rate design will have effects on
General Motors as a ratepayer, the only issues crucial to
the present determination are whether the effect on General
Motors could be material within the meaning of section
57103 and,
if so, whether the effect would he
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally.
The commission has elaborated on the statutory concept
of materiality in its regulation,
2 cal. Adm. code Section
la702 (copy enclosed).
This section proVides generally
that a significant effect is material.
2 Cal. Adm. code
Section 18702(a) .
it also sets forth the following
numerical guidelines to assist in determining the
materiality of an effect on a business entity.
(1)
whether,
in the case of a business entity in
which the public official holds a direct or indirect
investment of one thousand dollars (51,000)
or more or
in the case of a business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, employee,
trustee or holds any position of management,
the effect
of the decision will be to increase or decrease:
mi
The annualized gross revenues by the lesser
of:
1.
one hundred thousand dollars 4510043001; or
2.
One percent if it is one thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more: or


'
Steve Weissman
May 13,
1982
Page
5
(a)
Annual net income by the lesser of:
1.
Fifty thousand dollars (50,000};
or
2.
One half of one percent if it is one thousand
dollars (31,000) or more: or
c.
current assets or liabilities by the lesser
of:
1.
One hundred thousand dollars ($100,000); or
2.
one half of one percent if it is one thousand
dollars ($1,000)
or more.
current assets are deemed to be decreased by the
amount of any expenses incurred as a result of a
governmental decision.
Accordingly, in this matter, the decision on the
utility‘s rate design would have to have a $100,000 effect
on the expenses or gross revenues of General Motors in
order to be material.
a staff member at the code who worked on the staff
proposal indicated that the effect on General Motors' rates
would be less than 1:, an amount which over a year‘s period
would clearly fall short of $100,000.2/
Thus,
the effect
on General Motors is not material within the meaning of
Section 07103, and commissioner Grew-s participation is not
prohibited.
Although the effect was determined to not be material,
we also considered the issue of whether these kinds of
decisions fell within the "public generally" exception to
the disqualification statute.
This adVice is given
primarily to guide commissioner Grew in future matters
The "public generally" exception allows an official to
participate in a decision in which he or she has a
5/
We were not able to obtain the exact figures for
General Motors in the short time availaole to us.
for a 1\
effect to be material or $100,000, Gn's annual gas bill
would have to be :10 million dollars or more,
it seems
reasonable to assume,
however,
that General Motors'
annual
gas bill does not exceed 510 mlllion dollars.
if this
proves to be incorrect, then our conclusion would change
accordingly.


steve weissman
May is, 1932
Page a
financial interest if the decision will affect the public
generally or a significant segment thereof in the same
manner as it affects the official.
As we discussed,
there are
no hard and
fast
rules for
determining when a decision has an effect on a business
entity that is distinguishable from its effect on the
public generally.
However, there are factors which
influence the determination.
First, the size of the group
affected is significant.
clearly, a decision which affects
all residential ratepayers in a similar manner is a
decision which affects the public generally.
on
the other
hand,
a decision to place AD or so ratepayers
in a special
rate category or tier cannot be said to affect the public
generally.
other factors include the shared
characteristics of the group,
).Ei whether they are a
heterogenous group with varying interests and needs such as
a residential neighborhood or whether they are an industry
or professional group such as oil companies or doctors.
in view of the relatively large number and diversity of
commercial and industrial ratepayers,
we concluded that
they could he considered a significant segment of the
public generally for the purposes of conflicts
analysis.§/
Thus commissioner Grew can generally vote
in
all rate matters.
However,
if
a particular decision
potentially involves singling out a subgroup within this
group, it would be necessary to determine whether this
subgroup was also a significant segment of the public
generally.
In the particular situation presented here, it did not
appear that commercial and industrial users of over 2,500
therms per month constituted
a significant segment of
the
public generally.l/
it
is a very small subgroup
5/
We based this on the figures concerning southern
California Gas Company.
we can assume that comparable
statistics would be available for the service areas of
other large utilities.
however, in the case of small or
rural-based utilities,
this conclusion may not be valid.
In these cases,
it would have to be assessed on a
case-by-case basis.
1/
Assuming that most Gm cutomers use over 2,500
therms per month and that most GNl customers use under 300
tnerms per month,
i :oughl‘] calculated that 929 users
(270acommerCial, SSS-industrial) at the most use over 2,500
therms or .n of all industrial/commercial customers.


Steve Weissman
May 13,
1982
base 7
consisting only of the largest users.
Therefore,
it was
necessary, and would be necessary in similar future cases,
to determine whether an effect which applied only to this
subgroup was material.
1 hope this discussion has been helpful to you.
1 know
that this analysis often seems elusive, but it is necessary
to use a case-by-case analysis of all of the facts in this
area.
It
is difficult
to
formulate
rules which can be
applied
in all
situations.
I
remain available
to assist
You at your
[Equesfn
You also raised some procedural questions concerning
disqualification.
If an official has
a disqualifiying
financial
interest,
he of
she must abstain from all
participation
from
the point at which
the official
knows
or
has
reason
to know he or
she
has
a
financial
interest.
Prohibited participation in a decision for a disqualified
official includes participating or testifying at hearings,
presiding at a meeting where the matter
is discussed,
discussion with other officials,
negotiations,
and public
or Private comments
on
the matter
which are
intended
to
influence
the deciSioni
If Commissioner Grew
is ever disqualified
from
participating in a matter, you would not have
to disqualify
yourself
also based
on
her
financial
interest.
Ms.
Grew
could not attempt to participate in or influence the
decision through you, but you could fully participate
otherwise
as you normally
do.
If you would like to discuss this letter. please feel
free
to call me at
19l6)
322-590].
Very truly yours,
[Ag/tilmL/r
Diane Maura Fishbufn
Counsel
Legal Division
DMszlh

sections.question: Whether Commissioner Priscilla Grew has a conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act, Government Code Sections 87100 et seq., by participating in California Public Utilities Commission rate design proceedings for Southern California Gas Company, given her investment of more than $1,000 in General Motors, which participated in the hearings as a ratepayer and customer of the utility.
sections.conclusion: Commissioner Grew is not prohibited from participating in the pending rate design proceedings involving Southern California Gas Company. The effect on General Motors' rates would be less than 1%, which falls far short of the $100,000 benchmark used for determining materiality of an effect on business entities. Thus, the effect on General Motors is not material within the meaning of Section 87103, and Commissioner Grew's participation is not prohibited. Additionally, even if an effect were material, it would likely fall within the 'public generally' exception since the rate design changes would affect all ratepayers in a similar manner.

sections.question_synthetic: Does Commissioner Grew's investment in General Motors create a disqualifying conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act when she participates in CPUC rate design decisions for Southern California Gas Company, where General Motors is a major customer?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Commissioner Grew may participate in the SoCalGas rate design proceedings because any financial effect on General Motors would be less than 1% of the company's annual gas bill and therefore not material under Section 87103's $100,000 threshold for business entities. The rate design changes would affect all ratepayers similarly, potentially qualifying for the 'public generally' exception.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: modern; LLM: Document contains two letters dated April 30, 1982 and May 13, 1982 (with May 13 appearing to be the formal advice letter No. A-32-077). OCR quality is poor in places with some character recognition errors, but Q&C sections are clearly identifiable. The document includes detailed analysis of materiality standards under 2 Cal. Admin. Code Section 18702 and discusses both the materiality and 'public generally' exceptions. Minor date inconsistencies in OCR (1952 vs 1982), but context indicates 1982 is correct.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether Commissioner Priscilla Grew has a conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act, Government Code Sections 87100 et seq., by participating in California Public Utilities Commission rate design proceedings for Southern California Gas Company, given her investment of more than $1,000 in General Motors, which participated in the hearings as a ratepayer and customer of the utility.

CONCLUSION: Commissioner Grew is not prohibited from participating in the pending rate design proceedings involving Southern California Gas Company. The effect on General Motors' rates would be less than 1%, which falls far short of the $100,000 benchmark used for determining materiality of an effect on business entities. Thus, the effect on General Motors is not material within the meaning of Section 87103, and Commissioner Grew's participation is not prohibited. Additionally, even if an effect were material, it would likely fall within the 'public generally' exception since the rate design changes would affect all ratepayers in a similar manner.

embedding.summary: FPPC Advice Letter No. A-32-077 addresses whether Commissioner Priscilla Grew of the California Public Utilities Commission has a conflict of interest in participating in rate design proceedings for Southern California Gas Company, given her investment in General Motors, which testified as an interested party. The FPPC concludes that no disqualifying conflict exists because any financial effect on General Motors would be immaterial (less than 1% of its gas bill, far below the $100,000 materiality threshold) and would affect the public generally in the same manner.
